section.title: The Vision of the Glory of God
section.sectionRationale: The book opens not in Jerusalem but by a canal in Babylon, among a defeated and displaced people. There, of all places, the heavens open and a priest turned exile sees the glory of the LORD, mobile, sovereign, and undiminished, enthroned above a storm of living creatures and whirling wheels.
section.thesis: Even among the exiles by a foreign river, the glory of God appears enthroned and on the move, teaching Israel, and every reader since, that the LORD is not confined to a ruined temple or a lost land but reigns wherever His people are found.
section.frameNote: Five scenes: the priest among the exiles; the storm and the four living creatures; the wheels within wheels; the crystal expanse and the throne above it; and the appearance of the glory of the LORD.

units.label: A priest among the exiles
units.note: the heavens open by the Kebar

verses.text: In the thirtieth year, on the fifth day of the fourth month, while I was among the exiles by the River Kebar, the heavens opened and I saw visions of God.
On the fifth day of the month, it was the fifth year of the exile of King Jehoiachin,
the word of the LORD came directly to Ezekiel the priest, the son of Buzi, in the land of the Chaldeans by the River Kebar. And there the LORD's hand was upon him.

units.label: The storm and the living creatures
units.note: four faces, four wings, one movement

verses.text: I looked and saw a whirlwind coming from the north, a great cloud with fire flashing back and forth and brilliant light all around it. In the center of the fire was a gleam like {{chashmal|amber}},
and within it was the form of four living creatures. And this was their appearance: They had a human form,
but each had four faces and four wings.
Their legs were straight, and the soles of their feet were like the hooves of a calf, gleaming like polished bronze.
Under their wings on their four sides they had human hands. All four living creatures had faces and wings,
and their wings were touching one another. They did not turn as they moved; each one went straight ahead.
The form of their faces was that of a man, and each of the four had the face of a lion on the right side, the face of an ox on the left side, and also the face of an eagle.
Such were their faces. Their wings were spread upward; each had two wings touching the wings of the creature on either side, and two wings covering its body.
Each creature went straight ahead. Wherever the {{ruach|spirit}} would go, they would go, without turning as they moved.

In the midst of the living creatures was the appearance of glowing coals of fire, or of torches. Fire moved back and forth between the living creatures; it was bright, and lightning flashed out of it.
The creatures were darting back and forth as quickly as flashes of lightning.

units.label: Wheels within wheels
units.note: the spirit of the living creatures was in the wheels

verses.text: When I looked at the living creatures, I saw a wheel on the ground beside each creature with its four faces.
The workmanship of the wheels looked like the gleam of beryl, and all four had the same likeness. Their workmanship looked like a wheel within a wheel.
As they moved, they went in any of the four directions, without pivoting as they moved.
Their rims were high and awesome, and all four rims were full of eyes all around.
So as the living creatures moved, the wheels moved beside them, and when the creatures rose from the ground, the wheels also rose.
Wherever the spirit would go, they would go, and the wheels would rise alongside them, because the spirit of the living creatures was in the wheels.

When the creatures moved, the wheels moved; when the creatures stood still, the wheels stood still; and when the creatures rose from the ground, the wheels rose alongside them, because the spirit of the living creatures was in the wheels.

units.label: The expanse and the throne
units.note: a throne above the crystal expanse

verses.text: Spread out above the heads of the living creatures was the likeness of an awesome expanse, gleaming like crystal.
And under the expanse, their wings stretched out toward one another. Each one also had two wings covering its body.
When the creatures moved, I heard the sound of their wings like the roar of many waters, like the voice of the Almighty, like the tumult of an army. When they stood still, they lowered their wings.
And there came a voice from above the expanse over their heads as they stood still with their wings lowered.
Above the expanse over their heads was the likeness of a throne with the appearance of sapphire, and on the throne high above was a figure like that of a man.

units.label: The appearance of the glory of the LORD

verses.text: From what seemed to be His waist up, I saw a gleam like amber, with what looked like fire within it all around. And from what seemed to be His waist down, I saw what looked like fire; and brilliant light surrounded Him.

The appearance of the brilliant light all around Him was like that of a rainbow in a cloud on a rainy day. This was the appearance of the likeness of the {{kavod|glory}} of the LORD. And when I saw it, I fell facedown and heard a voice speaking.

kavod.gloss: Glory, weight, splendor, the visible manifest presence of God. Elsewhere the kavod fills the tabernacle and the temple; here it appears beside a canal in Babylon, teaching the exiles that the LORD's glory is not chained to Jerusalem's ruins but reigns wherever He chooses to reveal Himself.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, wind, breath. The same word describes the stormy wind out of the north and the living spirit that animates the creatures and the wheels, so that the whole moving throne is driven by God's own life-giving Spirit rather than by any mechanism.

chashmal.gloss: A rare and difficult word, traditionally rendered amber or gleaming metal, an alloy of fire and brightness. Its very obscurity fits the scene: Ezekiel reaches for the nearest ordinary words, amber, sapphire, crystal, to gesture at a glory that finally exceeds them all.

basic.context: Ezekiel is a priest, but he is not in the temple. He is a captive among captives, sitting by an irrigation canal in Babylon five years into the exile. There, of all unlikely places, the heavens open and he sees a vision of God: a storm from the north, four strange living creatures, wheels within wheels full of eyes, a crystal expanse, and above it all a throne with a figure like a man, blazing with light like a rainbow in the clouds.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real inaugural vision that commissions Ezekiel as a prophet, told in the layered, groping language apocalyptic visions use when human words strain to describe divine reality. Readers should hold the details, the faces, the wheels, the colors, with humility rather than pressing them into a technical blueprint; the vision's unmistakable center is that the glory of God is present, mobile, and sovereign even in exile.
basic.originalLanguages: The great word is kavod, glory, the weighty, visible presence of God, seen here far from Jerusalem. The wheels and creatures move by the ruach, the spirit or wind of God, and the flashing brightness at the heart of the fire is called chashmal, a rare Hebrew word usually rendered amber, itself a sign of glory too bright for ordinary language.
basic.typology: The mobile throne, resting on no fixed foundation, anticipates a presence of God no longer confined to one building. The four living creatures reappear around God's throne in Revelation 4, now joined by worship of the Lamb, and the man like a figure on the throne anticipates the day when God's glory is seen in a true man, Jesus Christ.
basic.christ: John writes that Isaiah, in his own throne vision, saw Christ's glory and spoke about Him, John 12:41; the same principle stands here. The kavod enthroned above the cherubim in Ezekiel 1 is the same glory the apostle John says was seen in the flesh in Jesus, who 'became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen His glory,' John 1:14. What Ezekiel glimpsed veiled in storm and fire, the church has seen unveiled in the face of Christ.
basic.god: God is transcendent and holy, so overwhelming that the prophet can only describe Him by likeness upon likeness, yet He is also present and near, appearing not in the temple courts but beside a ditch in a pagan empire. His throne is not fixed to one place; wherever His exiled people are, His glory can be found.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 6 pictures a similar throne vision at the prophet's call. Revelation 4 gathers four living creatures and a throne like this one before the Lamb. John 1:14 and 12:41 identify the glory of God, seen by the prophets, with the glory of Jesus Christ.
basic.oneStory: The story of the Bible is the story of God's glory dwelling with His people, lost through sin, exiled with Israel, and finally returned to dwell among us in Jesus Christ, whose glory outshines every vision, and who will one day fill the new creation the way the kavod once filled the tabernacle.

context.literary: The opening vision of the book, told in Ezekiel's own dense, repetitive, almost breathless style as he searches for words for what he saw. It sets the pattern for the whole book: visions of God's glory bracketing oracles of judgment and hope, and it establishes the mobile throne-chariot as the controlling image of God's presence throughout Ezekiel.
context.historical: Ezekiel was among the elite of Judah deported to Babylon in 597 BC with King Jehoiachin, years before Jerusalem's final fall in 586 BC. To exiles who assumed that losing the land and the temple meant losing God's presence, this vision announces the opposite: the glory that once filled Solomon's temple has come to them, in Babylon, by a foreign canal.
context.canonical: This vision stands alongside Isaiah 6 and Daniel 7 as one of Scripture's great throne visions, and it directly shapes the imagery of Revelation 4, where the four living creatures and the throne reappear around the risen and reigning Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish, at the very start of Ezekiel's ministry, that the glory of the LORD is sovereign, mobile, and present even among a defeated and exiled people, so that the prophet, and Israel through him, would know God had not abandoned them to Babylon.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vision describes a unique commissioning encounter; it does not prescribe a pattern for later visions or a code to be decoded piece by piece. Its abiding truth is theological: the reality and mobility of God's glory, not a blueprint of angelic biology or a chariot's mechanics.

debates.question: What are the four living creatures?

views.label: Cherubim, the same beings named later in the vision
views.case: Ezekiel himself identifies these creatures as cherubim in Ezekiel 10:20, the same order of angelic being that guarded Eden and overshadowed the ark, here attending God's throne in glory.

views.label: A symbol gathering all creation before God
views.case: The four faces, man, lion, ox, and eagle, are often read as representing humanity and the noblest of the wild beasts, cattle, and birds, so that all creation is pictured attending and serving the Creator's throne.

debates.bearing: Either reading agrees that the creatures exist to attend and display God's glory; the details serve worship and wonder, not zoological or angelological precision.

debates.question: What do the wheels signify?

views.label: God's total, unhindered sovereignty and presence
views.case: Wheels within wheels, moving in any direction without turning, full of eyes, dramatize that God's throne can go anywhere, see everything, and answer to no obstacle, even the deserts between Jerusalem and Babylon.

views.label: God's vigilant rule over history and nations
views.case: The eyes covering the rims are sometimes read as picturing the all-seeing oversight by which God governs the rise and fall of empires, Babylon included, so that nothing in world events escapes His notice or control.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge on the same pastoral point for exiles: no power on earth, not Babylon's armies nor its gods, can hem in or outrun the LORD who reigns from this throne.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter builds to one Hebrew word, kavod, glory, the visible weight of God's presence, and it is driven throughout by ruach, spirit or wind, the same word later used when God's Spirit stands Ezekiel on his feet, 2:2, and raises dry bones to life, 37:9-10. Even the strange word chashmal, amber, gestures at a brightness Hebrew itself struggles to name, reminding the reader that every description here is an approach toward a glory finally beyond words.

typology.type: The mobile throne of glory, unbound to one location
typology.fulfillment: God's presence no longer confined to a temple made with hands, fulfilled in the incarnation, John 1:14, and in the church as a living temple, and consummated when God dwells with His people without any temple at all, Revelation 21:22.

typology.type: The four living creatures before the throne
typology.fulfillment: The same four living creatures surround God's throne in Revelation 4:6-8, now joined in worship of the slain and risen Lamb in Revelation 5.

typology.type: A figure like a man enthroned above the cherubim
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the day the eternal Word takes on true humanity and is exalted to the throne of glory, Philippians 2:9-11.

study.christ: John's Gospel says of the prophet who saw the LORD high and lifted up on a throne, 'Isaiah said these things because he saw His glory and spoke about Him,' John 12:41, meaning the glory the prophets saw was, in some real sense, the glory of Christ. What Ezekiel witnessed veiled in whirlwind, fire, and rainbow light is the same kavod John says the church has seen with its own eyes: 'The Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen His glory, the glory of the one and only Son, full of grace and truth,' John 1:14. The mobile throne, unbound to any single place, points forward to a presence of God that would one day tabernacle among us in a person rather than in a building.
study.god: God is holy and utterly other, so that even a priest trained in temple protocol has no vocabulary but a cascade of likenesses, 'as it were,' 'like the appearance of,' to describe Him. Yet this transcendent God is not distant; His glory travels to a canal bank in a pagan empire to meet one exiled priest. He is sovereign over Babylon's gods and armies alike, and His throne answers to no geography.

crossReferences.note: Another prophetic throne vision, high and lifted up, with seraphim and the cry of holiness.
Later identifies the four living creatures explicitly as cherubim.
The four living creatures and the throne reappear in John's vision of heaven.
The glory of God, once seen by prophets in vision, is beheld in the incarnate Christ.

study.oneStory: The Bible's story is the story of God's glory dwelling with His people: given in Eden, housed in the tabernacle and temple, seemingly lost with the exile, yet here revealed to a defeated priest beside a foreign canal as mobile, sovereign, and undiminished. That same glory is finally revealed in Jesus Christ, who tabernacles among us in flesh, and will one day fill the new creation the way the kavod once filled the tent in the wilderness.

sermon.title: The God Who Speaks
sermon.why: Begg preaches directly from Ezekiel 1:1-28, drawing out how the overwhelming, mobile vision of God's glory meets a prophet in exile and still speaks to a God who is never confined by our circumstances.

quiz.q: Where was Ezekiel when he saw this vision of God's glory?

options.text: In the Jerusalem temple during a festival.
options.note: He is a priest, but he is far from the temple, in exile.

options.text: Among the exiles by the River Kebar in Babylon.
options.note: Ezekiel 1:1-3 sets the vision in exile, not in Jerusalem.

options.text: Traveling toward Egypt with refugees.
options.note: The text places him in Babylon, not Egypt.

options.text: Nowhere in particular; the setting is not given.
options.note: The setting by the River Kebar is stated clearly.

quiz.q: What moved the living creatures and the wheels together?

options.text: The wind blowing them along the ground.
options.note: The storm wind opens the vision, but the spirit within governs the movement.

options.text: Nothing; they stood still throughout.
options.note: The creatures and wheels move together repeatedly in the vision.

options.text: The spirit, or ruach, of the living creatures was in the wheels.
options.note: Ezekiel 1:20-21 says the spirit of the living creatures was in the wheels.

options.text: A system of gears and axles.
options.note: The vision describes spirit-driven movement, not mechanical parts.

quiz.q: What did Ezekiel see above the crystal expanse over the creatures' heads?

options.text: The ark of the covenant.
options.note: The vision describes a throne in the heavens, not the ark.

options.text: A second set of living creatures.
options.note: It is a throne with a figure upon it, not more creatures.

options.text: An empty expanse with no throne.
options.note: The throne and its occupant are explicitly seen.

options.text: The likeness of a throne, with a figure like a man seated above it.
options.note: Ezekiel 1:26 describes the throne and the human-like figure upon it.

quiz.q: How did Ezekiel respond when he saw the appearance of the glory of the LORD?

options.text: He fell facedown and heard a voice speaking.
options.note: Ezekiel 1:28 records this response of awe and submission.

options.text: He ran away from the river.
options.note: The text describes him falling facedown, not fleeing.

options.text: He argued with the vision.
options.note: His response was worshipful awe, not argument.

options.text: He felt nothing unusual.
options.note: The vision overwhelms him; his response is dramatic, not indifferent.

quiz.q: What does this vision teach the exiles about God's presence?

options.text: God's presence had been left behind in Jerusalem.
options.note: The opposite is true; the glory appears in Babylon itself.

options.text: The exiles were beyond God's reach.
options.note: The vision insists God reaches and reigns even in exile.

options.text: God's glory is mobile and sovereign, not confined to the ruined temple in Jerusalem.
options.note: The whole vision, especially the wheels, portrays a throne that travels and reigns anywhere.

options.text: God only speaks through dreams, never in visible glory.
options.note: This is an actual seen vision of glory, not merely a dream.

questions: Ezekiel saw the glory of God not in the temple but beside a canal among defeated exiles. Where in your own life do you assume God is absent simply because your circumstances look like defeat?

The throne in this vision moves freely in every direction. What would change in your prayers this week if you truly believed God's presence and rule are not confined to your best or most comfortable seasons?

Ezekiel reaches for word after word, 'like,' 'as it were,' to describe what he saw. How does approaching God with humility and wonder, rather than certainty about every detail, shape the way you read Scripture's harder visions?
The wheels were full of eyes, suggesting nothing escapes God's notice. Is there an area of your life or your world's events that you struggle to believe is under God's watchful, sovereign care?
John says the prophets who saw God's glory were in some sense seeing Christ. How does knowing that Jesus is the fullest revelation of the very glory Ezekiel saw change the way you look at Him?

translationNotes.note: The word rendered amber, chashmal, is rare and its precise meaning is uncertain; ancient and modern translators alike have rendered it amber, electrum, or simply gleaming metal.

Spirit here may equally be rendered wind; the same Hebrew word, ruach, carries both senses, and the ambiguity is likely intentional, joining the storm wind to the animating spirit within the creatures.
Sapphire may refer to the gemstone we know or, as some scholars suggest, to lapis lazuli, a deep blue stone well known in the ancient Near East.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 1:1-28. Em dashes in verse 2 replaced with commas; embedded line breaks in verses 5, 11, and 24 flattened into continuous prose. No em or en dashes used.
